component.name: Button
component.description: An interactive element that triggers an action when activated. Buttons communicate what will happen when the user interacts with them and are the primary mechanism for initiating actions within a surface.

web-component.note: Available as a Web Component wrapper. Native shadow DOM implementation planned for v4.

android.note: Compose implementation in progress. Expected in v4.0.

lastUpdated.note: Added focus-visible guidance and refreshed contrast requirements for inverse surfaces.

metadata.summary: An interactive element that triggers an action when activated.

items.description: Web Component wrapper. Available since v3.2.0 — native shadow DOM implementation planned for v4.

documentBlocks.description: The Button is composed of a container, a text label, and an optional leading or trailing icon.

parts.name: Container
parts.description: The outer boundary of the button. Receives background color, border, border radius, and padding. Defines the clickable area.

parts.name: Label
parts.description: The text content of the button. Communicates the action that will occur on activation.

parts.name: Icon
parts.description: An optional icon displayed before (leading) or after (trailing) the label. Reinforces the label's meaning visually.

parts.name: Focus Ring
parts.description: A visible outline rendered when the button receives keyboard focus. Not displayed on mouse interaction.

presentation.alt: An annotated diagram of a primary button with numbered callouts: 1. Container, 2. Label, 3. Icon (optional), 4. Focus ring (shown in dashed outline).

properties.description: The visual style of the button. Determines background color, text color, and border treatment.
The size of the button. Affects padding, font size, icon size, and minimum target area.
When true, the button is non-interactive. The cursor changes to not-allowed, and the button is visually dimmed to 40% opacity.
When true, the label is replaced with a spinner and the button is non-interactive. The button retains its dimensions to prevent layout shift.
When true, the button expands to fill the width of its parent container.
An icon component rendered before the label. When provided without a label, an aria-label is required.
An icon component rendered after the label.
The HTML button type attribute. Controls form submission behavior.

events.description: Fires when the button is activated via mouse click, touch tap, Enter key, or Space key. Does not fire when the button is disabled or loading. Bubbles; not cancelable.
Fires when the button receives focus via keyboard tab, programmatic focus, or mouse click. Use to show contextual help or tooltips. Does not bubble.
Fires when the button loses focus. Use to dismiss contextual help or validate inline state. Does not bubble.

slots.acceptedContent: Plain text or a text node. Do not nest interactive elements, headings, or block-level elements.

cssCustomProperties.description: The background color of the button container.
The color of the label text.
The border radius of the button container.

dataAttributes.description: Reflects the current interactive state of the button. Useful for styling with attribute selectors.
Reflects the current variant. Useful for parent-level conditional styling.

items.name: Emphasis
items.description: Controls the visual weight of the button. Determines background fill, border treatment, and text color to establish a visual hierarchy among actions on a surface.

values.name: Primary
values.description: High-emphasis — the main action on the surface. Uses a solid, filled background. Limit to one primary button per surface.
values.rationale:
- Use when: When the action is the most important on the surface — the one the user is most likely to take (e.g., Save, Submit, Confirm).
- Avoid when: When a surface already has a primary button. Adding a second dilutes visual hierarchy. Use `secondary` instead — Secondary emphasis maintains importance without competing with the existing primary action.

values.name: Secondary
values.description: Medium-emphasis — important but not the primary action. Uses a visible border and transparent background.
values.rationale: - Use when: When the action is important but secondary to a primary action on the same surface (e.g., Cancel alongside Save).

values.name: Ghost
values.description: Low-emphasis — tertiary actions, toolbar actions, or dense layouts. No background or border in the default state.
values.rationale:
- Use when: When the action is tertiary or supplementary — helpful but not essential to the user's primary task.
- Avoid when: When the action is the only action on the surface and needs to be clearly discoverable. Use `secondary` instead — A ghost button on its own can be overlooked. Secondary emphasis provides enough visual presence to be discoverable.

values.name: Danger
values.description: High-emphasis destructive — signals an irreversible action. Uses the danger color. Pair with a confirmation dialog.
values.rationale:
- Use when: When the action is destructive or irreversible — deleting a record, revoking access, removing a team member.
- Avoid when: When the action is not destructive, even if it feels important or urgent. Use `primary` instead — The danger color is a strong signal reserved for destruction. Using it for non-destructive actions dilutes its meaning.

items.name: Size
items.description: Controls the physical dimensions of the button — padding, font size, icon size, and minimum touch target area.

values.name: Small
values.description: Compact size for toolbars and dense layouts. 32px height.

values.name: Medium
values.description: Default size for most contexts. 40px height.

values.name: Large
values.description: Touch-optimized size for mobile-first surfaces. 48px height.

items.name: Default
items.description: The button's resting state when no interaction is occurring.

items.name: Hover
items.description: Triggered when the user's pointer moves over the button. The background darkens by 8% to indicate interactivity. Not applicable on touch devices.

items.name: Active / Pressed
items.description: Triggered while the button is being pressed (mousedown or touch start). The background darkens by 16% from the default to indicate activation.

items.name: Focus
items.description: Triggered when the button receives keyboard focus. A 2px focus ring appears with a 2px offset from the container edge.

items.name: Disabled
items.description: The button is non-interactive. Opacity is reduced to 0.4. Pointer events are disabled. The button remains in the tab order when using aria-disabled instead of the HTML disabled attribute.

items.name: Loading
items.description: The button label is replaced by a spinner animation. The button is non-interactive. The button maintains its dimensions from the default state to prevent layout shift.

responsive.description: In narrow containers (below 320px), buttons expand to full width automatically to maintain a usable tap target.
Buttons display at their intrinsic width. Button groups display inline.

items.description: When the user needs to trigger an action such as submitting a form, saving data, opening a dialog, or confirming a decision.
When a destructive or irreversible action needs to be initiated, such as deleting a record or revoking access. Pair with a confirmation dialog.
When the action navigates the user to a different page or URL.

alternative.rationale: Links carry native navigation semantics. Screen readers announce them as links, and browsers support standard navigation behaviors such as open-in-new-tab.

items.description: When the user needs to select one option from a set of mutually exclusive choices.

alternative.rationale: Radio groups communicate exclusivity through their semantic role. A set of buttons styled to look like a selector does not convey mutual exclusivity to assistive technology.

items.description: When the only content is an icon with no visible text label.

alternative.rationale: Icon buttons enforce an aria-label requirement and apply size adjustments for icon-only touch targets. A standard button with its label removed may fail accessibility requirements silently.

items.guidance: Limit each surface to one primary button.
items.rationale: Multiple primary buttons dilute visual hierarchy. When everything is emphasized, nothing is. A single primary button directs the user to the most important action.

items.guidance: Place the primary button on the right side of a button group in left-to-right layouts.
items.rationale: Users scan in the direction of the layout's reading order. Placing the primary action at the natural endpoint aligns with the completion point of reading.

items.guidance: Do not use a Button when the action navigates the user to a different page or URL. Use a Link component instead.
items.rationale: Buttons and links have different semantic roles. Buttons trigger actions (submit, open, close). Links navigate. Screen reader users rely on element role to anticipate behavior.

items.guidance: Use the danger variant exclusively for destructive or irreversible actions. Pair danger buttons with a confirmation dialog.
items.rationale: Red is a strong signal. If danger styling is used for non-destructive actions, it dilutes the warning signal and conditions users to ignore it.

items.guidance: Use the loading state instead of disabling the button during asynchronous operations.
items.rationale: A disabled button gives no feedback that an action is in progress. The loading state communicates that the action was registered and the system is working.

items.guidance: Do not wrap a button's label text across multiple lines.
items.rationale: Multi-line button labels are harder to scan and create inconsistent button heights in groups. If the label is too long, rewrite it to be shorter.

items.guidance: Maintain a minimum tap target of 44x44 CSS pixels for all button sizes.
items.rationale: The WCAG 2.5.8 target size criterion requires a minimum 24x24px target, with 44x44px recommended. Touch devices require larger targets to prevent mis-taps.

items.guidance: Use a verb or verb phrase that describes the action the button performs. Two words maximum.
items.rationale: Action-oriented labels set clear expectations about what will happen on activation. Short labels prevent truncation on narrow viewports.
Sentence case is easier to read than title case or all caps. It also localizes more predictably across languages where capitalization rules differ.

items.guidance: When using an icon-only button (no visible label), provide an aria-label that describes the action.
items.rationale: Screen readers announce button content as the accessible name. Without visible text, there is no accessible name. The aria-label provides one.

items.guidance: Use the native <button> element. Do not recreate button behavior on a <div> or <span>.
items.rationale: Native buttons provide built-in keyboard interaction (Enter, Space), focus management, and form submission behavior. Recreating this on a non-semantic element is error-prone.

items.guidance: Prefer aria-disabled="true" over the HTML disabled attribute when the button should remain discoverable by screen reader users.
items.rationale: The HTML disabled attribute removes the button from the tab order, making it invisible to keyboard users. aria-disabled keeps the button focusable and announceable.

items.guidance: The focus ring must be visible in all color modes (light, dark, high contrast).
items.rationale: Keyboard users depend on the focus indicator to track their position. If the focus ring is invisible against the background, navigation becomes impossible.

items.guidance: Button label text must meet a minimum 4.5:1 contrast ratio against the button background.
items.rationale: Text contrast ensures readability for users with low vision.

keyboardInteractions.action: Moves focus to the next focusable element in the tab order.
Moves focus to the previous focusable element in the tab order.

ariaAttributes.description: Applied automatically by the <button> element. Only set explicitly when using the 'as' prop to render a non-button element.
Set to 'true' when the button is non-interactive. Preferred over the HTML disabled attribute when the button should remain focusable for screen reader discoverability.
Provides an accessible name for icon-only buttons that lack visible text. Not needed when a visible label is present.
Set to 'true' when the button is in the loading state. Communicates to assistive technology that the button's action is in progress.

colorContrast.context: Label text on primary button background in light mode.
Secondary button border/text against the default page background in light mode.
Label text on danger button background in light mode.

focusBehaviors.behavior: Participates in the normal tab order; does not trap or redirect focus.
Focus moves to the opened element — the modal or popover component owns that move.

reducedMotion.behavior: Replaced with a static ellipsis indicator.

agentDocumentBlocks.purpose: Trigger a user-initiated action within the current view without causing page navigation.

items.description: Use button for in-page actions; use link for navigation to a URL.
Use button when a visible text label is present; use icon-button for icon-only affordances.

items.guidance: Do not use for navigating to a different page or URL.
items.evidence: Navigation-via-button flagged in 8/10 accessibility audits across design systems.

items.guidance: Limit each surface to one primary-emphasis button.

examples.description: Two primary buttons on the same surface dilute visual hierarchy.

presentation.code:
<!-- Wrong: two primary buttons -->
<Button variant="primary">Save</Button>
<Button variant="primary">Cancel</Button>

<!-- Correct: one primary, one secondary -->
<Button variant="primary">Save</Button>
<Button variant="secondary">Cancel</Button>

items.guidance: Always provide an accessible label via visible text or aria-label.
Using a button to navigate to another page.
items.rationale: Instead: Use a link element with href.
items.evidence: Most common agent error in UI generation benchmarks.

items.guidance: Placing multiple primary buttons on the same surface.
items.rationale: Instead: Use one primary and one or more secondary or tertiary buttons.

items.label: Use a single primary-emphasis button per surface.
Give every icon-only button an accessible name via aria-label.
items.description: The label should describe the action, not the icon (e.g., 'Close dialog').

items.label: Use a link, not a button, for navigation to a different page or URL.
Confirm the touch target is at least 44×44 px on coarse-pointer devices.
Provide a loading state for buttons that trigger async actions.
